Party_Details.Name: Rafiq Jumabhoy

Facts.Text: Rafiq Jumabhoy was a director of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd ('SIS') appointed by board resolution to review investments in subsidiary companies[cite: 15, 25, 30].
A long-standing public family dispute existed between various members of the Jumabhoy family regarding the control and management of family companies[cite: 19].
The board of SIS passed resolutions authorizing the individual to take actions to preserve the company and indemnified him for 'all costs and expenses incurred'[cite: 25, 38].
The individual wrote to another director offering services for a fixed monthly pay of $25,000, which he alleged was orally accepted, though this was refuted in testimony [cite: 46-51].
Following the liquidation of the company, the individual claimed $916,275 for time costs (at $2,850 per day) and $161,934.68 for legal/travel costs[cite: 41, 44].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a board resolution indemnifying a director for 'costs and expenses' includes payment for his professional fees/time costs or payment on a quantum meruit basis[cite: 13, 16, 60].
Party_Details.Rule: Words in a board resolution must be construed in their specific context[cite: 14, 61]. In the context of director indemnities, 'costs and expenses' ordinarily refers to out-of-pocket expenses and does not include remuneration unless specifically approved by the board [cite: 68-69]. Directors are generally not entitled to quantum meruit payments for their services[cite: 70].
Party_Details.Application: The court found that the company was in financial difficulty at the time of the resolution, making it unlikely the board would have agreed to high professional fees without debate[cite: 62, 66]. There was no evidence that the board discussed or intended 'costs and expenses' to cover anything beyond out-of-pocket disbursements[cite: 67]. The Plaintiff failed to prove that the terms of his initial offer ($25,000/month) were incorporated into the resolution [cite: 51-52, 72].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected the dictionary-based interpretation that 'costs' included time value [cite: 57-59]. Relying on 'In re Richmond Gate Property Co Ltd', the judge held that director remuneration requires specific approval [cite: 69-70]. As the burden of proof to show an 'unusual meaning' for the resolution was not discharged, the claim for fees was dismissed, while an inquiry was ordered for legitimate out-of-pocket expenses[cite: 72, 86, 89].

Party_Details.Name: Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd (in compulsory liquidation)

Facts.Text: The company was a holding company for various shares and subsidiaries, which was placed under receivership and eventually compulsory liquidation [cite: 12, 15, 21-23].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the company is liable to pay a director for time-based management charges under an indemnity clause for 'costs and expenses'[cite: 13, 60].
Party_Details.Rule: Remuneration for directors must be clearly and specifically approved by a board resolution.
Party_Details.Application: The company (represented by liquidators) successfully argued that the resolution only covered actual expenses incurred on behalf of the entity[cite: 16, 88]. The company successfully refuted the allegation that an oral agreement for fixed monthly pay existed[cite: 51].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The company was found liable only for the reimbursement of actual out-of-pocket expenses, subject to an inquiry to determine if any such costs were in fact incurred[cite: 89, 91]. The company was not liable for the director's time-cost claim[cite: 87].
